backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text: The apex of the axilla is the cervicoaxillary canal, a triangular gateway bounded by the clavicle anteriorly, first rib medially, and superior border of the scapula posteriorly. All major neurovascular structures enter the axilla through this narrow passage. Clinical pearl: A Pancoast tumour at the lung apex can compress structures passing through this canal.

The anterior wall is formed by pectoralis major (superficial) and pectoralis minor (deep), along with the clavipectoral fascia. Pectoralis minor divides the axillary artery into its three parts. Memory aid: Think "Pec Sandwich" — major in front, minor behind, fascia filling the gaps.

The posterior wall is formed by subscapularis (upper), latissimus dorsi, and teres major (lower). The quadrangular and triangular spaces are found here, transmitting the axillary nerve and vessels. Memory aid: "SLT" — Subscapularis, Latissimus, Teres major, from top to bottom.

The medial wall is formed by upper ribs (1-4) and intercostal muscles, covered by serratus anterior. The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) runs along this wall. Clinical pearl: Damage to the long thoracic nerve causes winging of the scapula — ask the patient to push against a wall to test.

The lateral wall is the narrowest wall, formed by the intertubercular sulcus of the humerus. The axillary neurovascular bundle courses along this wall toward the arm. Clinical pearl: Anterior shoulder dislocation can injure the axillary nerve here, causing regimental badge anaesthesia.
